figures.gloss: The Supreme Being incarnate in Emperor Haile Selassie I of Ethiopia. He is the living God of the Black diaspora, the conquering Lion of the Tribe of Judah, who brings redemption to the oppressed and sits on the throne of King David.
figures.facet: Unlike the white, sky-dwelling God of colonial Christianity, Jah is a living, Black African monarch of the modern era. His divinity is not deferred to an afterlife; it is immanent and historically verified.
figures.provenance: The Holy Piby; Kebra Nagast; oral teachings of early preachers like Leonard Howell.

figures.gloss: The oppressive, materialistic, and corrupt western power structure, originally embodied by colonial Britain and the papacy, but encompassing the entire global capitalist system. It is the spiritual and physical site of Black exile and systemic bondage.
figures.facet: It is not a mythological underworld or a spiritual demon, but a present sociopolitical reality—a state machinery of police, politicians, and economic exploitation that must be actively resisted and fled.
figures.provenance: Psalms 137; Revelation; reggae lyricism; foundational Rastafari reasoning sessions.

figures.gloss: The sacred homeland and spiritual epicenter for all Black people, geographically located in Ethiopia and the broader African continent. It represents ultimate liberation, natural living, and the promised land of return where systemic oppression ceases.
figures.facet: Zion is not a post-mortem heavenly paradise but a tangible, geographical continent. Salvation means physical repatriation to African soil in this lifetime, violently rejecting the colonial promise of heaven after death.
figures.provenance: The Kebra Nagast; Hebrew Bible; Garveyite pan-African discourse.

figures.gloss: The pioneering Pan-African leader revered as a major prophet. He prophesied the crowning of a Black king in Africa, laying the ideological and spiritual groundwork for the movement and sparking the global consciousness of Black repatriation.
figures.facet: Garvey is venerated despite his own historical rejection of Rastafari. His political speeches and Black nationalist organizing were retroactively canonized as infallible divine prophecy by the early Rastafari patriarchs.
figures.provenance: Universal Negro Improvement Association (UNIA) speeches; oral traditions of early Rastafari.

figures.gloss: The core philosophical concept of divine unity. It expresses the unbroken, immanent presence of Jah within every individual, destroying the boundary between the human subject and the divine creator, and affirming radical equality among all living people.
figures.facet: It abolishes the grammatical and spiritual subjugation of 'you' and 'me'. By speaking 'I and I', the Rastaman constantly affirms that God is not separate from the self, disrupting colonial hierarchies of worth.
figures.provenance: Rastafari reasoning sessions (groundations); Dread Talk (Iyaric) linguistic philosophy.

figures.gloss: The sacred marijuana plant, viewed as the 'weed of wisdom' grown on the tomb of King Solomon. It is smoked as a religious sacrament to burn away corruption, facilitate deep meditation, and commune directly with Jah.
figures.facet: Unlike a symbolic ritual wafer or wine, ganja is a psychoactive catalyst for 'reasoning'. It is explicitly an illegal, criminalized plant that physically alters consciousness to break the mental programming of Babylon.
figures.provenance: Genesis 1:29; Revelation 22:2; Indo-Jamaican cultural exchange; Rastafari oral tradition.

figures.gloss: The foundational, heartbeat-rhythm drumming, chanting, and spiritual gathering of Rastafari. Named after a legendary Ugandan warrior queen, it is an invocation of divine justice designed to destroy oppression and Babylon through sheer acoustic and spiritual force.
figures.facet: It merges music, theology, and anti-colonial warfare. Nyabinghi is not merely worship; it is an active sonic weapon, where the rhythmic heartbeat is believed to physically and spiritually dismantle the walls of Babylon.
figures.provenance: East African anti-colonial history (Muhumusa); Jamaican Burru drumming; Mansions of Rastafari traditions.

figures.gloss: The divine feminine counterpart to Jah, incarnate in Empress Menen Asfaw of Ethiopia. She represents the Mother of Creation, the balancing root of the earth, and the cosmic archetype of the righteous Black woman.
figures.facet: While the movement can be highly patriarchal, Queen Omega establishes an inescapable divine dyad. King Alpha cannot rule without Queen Omega, rooting salvation in a married, historical African partnership rather than a solitary male deity.
figures.provenance: The Royal Parchment Scroll of Black Supremacy; The Promised Key (Leonard Howell).

figures.gloss: Jamaican preacher (1898-1981) who from 1933 preached Haile Selassie's divinity and, under the pseudonym G.G. Maragh, authored 'The Promised Key' (1935), the movement's founding tract. In 1940 he established the Pinnacle commune in St. Catherine, a self-sufficient Rastafari settlement repeatedly raided and finally broken up by police in 1954, scattering its members into the Kingston slums where Rastafari took urban root.
figures.facet: The movement's theology and its geography both begin with him: Pinnacle's destruction is the hinge event that turned a rural commune's doctrine into a citywide, then global, current.
figures.provenance: Leonard Howell (as G.G. Maragh), 'The Promised Key' (1935); Robert A. Hill, 'Dread History: Leonard Howell and Millenarian Visions in Early Rastafari Religion' (1981); Barry Chevannes, 'Rastafari: Roots and Ideology' (1994).

figures.gloss: The Ethiopian national epic of Solomon, the Queen of Sheba, and their son Menelik I, who brings the Ark of the Covenant to Ethiopia. Rastafari doctrine reads it as documentary proof that the Solomonic line — and with it the throne of David — resides in Ethiopia, underwriting Haile Selassie's titles and, through them, his divinity.
figures.facet: A fourteenth-century Ge'ez court chronicle is read outside Ethiopia as a live legal instrument: the deed to a throne, not a piece of national literature.
figures.provenance: The Kebra Nagast (14th-c. Ge'ez compilation); Chevannes, 'Rastafari: Roots and Ideology' (1994), on its doctrinal use among early preachers.

figures.gloss: The movement's organizational reality: chiefly the Nyahbinghi Order (the oldest priesthood, centered on Africa-repatriation ritual), the Bobo Ashanti (EABIC, founded by Prince Emmanuel Charles Edwards), and the Twelve Tribes of Israel — houses that differ on Selassie's precise status, dress, and discipline, taking their name from 'in my Father's house are many mansions' (John 14:2).
figures.facet: Rastafari is not one undifferentiated faith but a federation of houses that disagree with each other on real doctrinal points while sharing Jah, Babylon, and Zion as common ground.
figures.provenance: Chevannes (1994); Ennis Barrington Edmonds, 'Rastafari: From Outcasts to Culture Bearers' (2003).

figures.gloss: The 'vital' livity code governing food and daily life: natural, minimally processed, generally saltless and fleshless eating, kept to preserve the body as a clean temple against Babylon's chemical and commercial pollutions.
figures.facet: Where the ganja sacrament is occasional and ecstatic, Ital is the quiet, constant discipline — resistance to Babylon practiced three times a day at the table rather than in the reasoning circle.

figures.gloss: The uncut, uncombed hair worn as covenant, crown, and lion's mane, grounded scripturally in the Nazirite vow of Numbers 6:5 ('no razor shall come upon his head'). It is the tradition's most visible embodied refusal of Babylon's grooming and dress order.
figures.facet: A biblical vow of separation, taken by an ancient Israelite for a fixed term, is reworked into a permanent, public, racially coded sign of belonging — the covenant made visible on the body for life.

figures.gloss: The holy day commemorating 21 April 1966, when Haile Selassie I visited Jamaica and tens of thousands met him at Palisadoes airport. It is kept annually with nyabinghi drumming as the day the living God-King stood on Jamaican soil.
figures.facet: Unlike a mythic feast day, Grounation Day commemorates a dated, photographed, witnessed event within living memory — the tradition's central holy day is also a matter of twentieth-century public record.
figures.provenance: Barrett (1977/1997); Chevannes (1994), on the 1966 Selassie visit and its annual commemoration.

figures.gloss: Founder (1958) of the Ethiopia Africa Black International Congress (EABIC), better known as Bobo Ashanti, and convener that same year of the first all-island Rastafari convention. Within Bobo Ashanti he is revered as the Black Christ, completing a trinity with Selassie as King and Garvey as Prophet.
figures.facet: Where Selassie's divinity was found in a living foreign monarch, Bobo Ashanti locates a second incarnation inside the movement itself — the prophet who organizes the church becomes, for his own house, the god who indwells it.
figures.provenance: Edmonds (2003), on Bobo Ashanti/EABIC; Chevannes (1994), on the 1958 convention.

edges.note: Jah represents ultimate life, freedom, and divine truth, standing in total systemic opposition to the death and bondage of Babylon.
King Alpha and Queen Omega form the supreme divine dyad, demonstrating that perfect rule requires balanced male and female energies.
GOLD SEAM: Jah is a direct linguistic and theological inheritance of the Biblical Yahweh, re-contextualized into a living Black messiah.
GOLD SEAM: Haile Selassie is explicitly worshipped as the returned Christ in his Kingly character, fulfilling biblical prophecy.
GOLD SEAM: Both represent an oppressive, illegitimate architectural system designed to trap divine sparks in a realm of suffering and illusion.
GOLD SEAM: A pervasive, illusion-generating force that binds humanity to a corrupt material cycle, which must be overcome through enlightenment.
GOLD SEAM: The primordial, uncorrupted paradise of human origin, envisioned not just as a mythic past but a geographic destiny.
GOLD SEAM: The absolute spiritual and physical center of the universe, serving as the orienting homeland for the righteous.
GOLD SEAM: The localized fragment of the supreme creator dwelling directly within the human vessel, overriding worldly oppression.
GOLD SEAM: The supreme, unifying reality that dissolves the illusion of separation between the self and the cosmos.
GOLD SEAM: While Buddhism dissolves the self into non-self to end suffering, Rastafari elevates the self into the absolute 'I' to conquer oppression.
GOLD SEAM: The essential divine feminine consort who anchors and completes the supreme male deity in a cosmic partnership.
GOLD SEAM: A physiological and communal technology used to enter an ecstatic state, heal the community, and contact the divine.
GOLD SEAM: The prophetic forerunner who heralds the imminent arrival of a final, triumphant divine order that will remake the world.
GOLD SEAM: The overarching system of total negativity, destruction, and spiritual falsehood that wages war against the forces of light.
GOLD SEAM: A rhythmic, communal ritual meant to re-align the community with cosmic justice and ancestral truth.
GOLD SEAM: Rastafari discourse explicitly names the papacy an organ of Babylon — one tradition's world-axis is the other's world-prison, an attested polemical identification, not a projection.

GOLD SEAM: The Kebra Nagast's Solomonic charter is the documentary spine of Haile Selassie's throne titles ('Conquering Lion of the Tribe of Judah'), and Rastafari reads the emperor's divinity directly through this Ethiopian text — an attested textual transmission.

GOLD SEAM: Both relocate Zion to Ethiopia — the Tewahedo church by claiming the Ark at Aksum, Rastafari by naming Ethiopia the promised land; kindred Ethiopianist claims rather than one documented chain.
Zion relocated onto the believer's own continent — a Limpopo holy city and an Ethiopian promised land, two African re-plantings of the same biblical mountain.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Cherry was a documented Garvey supporter, folding UNIA-era Black nationalism directly into his 1886 congregation's theology — an attested historical link, not a structural analogy.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Matthew's Harlem preaching drew explicitly on the Garveyite pan-Africanism and Black nationalism circulating in the same 1920s streets Garvey's UNIA organized — an attested shared milieu, not mere resemblance.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: both diasporic movements read an ancestral homeland as a literal Zion to which the chosen must physically return — Ben Ammi's community enacted it via Liberia to Dimona, Rastafari holds it as aspirational repatriation to Ethiopia/Africa.

Two turn-of-the-century Jamaican mass movements — Bedward's Revivalist following and Garvey's Pan-Africanism — whose overlapping millennialism and confrontation with colonial authority both fed the ferment from which Rastafari emerged in the 1930s (Chevannes, Rastafari: Roots and Ideology, 1994).
Howell was the earliest and most consequential preacher of Selassie's divinity, author of the movement's founding tract.
The Promised Key is a named source for Queen Omega alongside the Royal Parchment Scroll — Howell's tract carries the divine-feminine doctrine as well as Jah's.
The Kebra Nagast's Solomon-Sheba-Menelik narrative is the documentary charter Rastafari reads directly as proof Jah's throne sits in Ethiopia.
The Nyahbinghi Order, one of the three Mansions, gives its name to the drumming and gathering already rostered.
Prince Emmanuel Charles Edwards founded Bobo Ashanti, one of the three houses this concept names.
Bobo Ashanti's trinity holds Selassie as King alongside Edwards as the Black Christ.
Bobo Ashanti's trinity completes with Garvey as Prophet, Edwards as Christ, Selassie as King.
The two most visible embodied covenants of Rastafari livity — uncut hair and the holy herb — together mark the Rastaman's body against Babylon.
Grounation Day is kept annually with nyabinghi drumming, its central historical commemoration.
Twentieth-century founder-prophets each elevated to divine or quasi-divine status within their own movement's cosmology, alongside a separate supreme figure.
The two halves of daily livity — what enters the body and how the body is worn — read together as the lived discipline beside the ganja sacrament and the Nyabinghi rite.

dossier.essence: Rastafari is a religious and Pan-African movement that arose among Afro-Jamaicans in the early 1930s after the 1930 coronation of Ras Tafari Makonnen as Emperor Haile Selassie I of Ethiopia. Its adherents hold that Selassie is the divine — Jah incarnate, the returned Christ in his kingly character — and that Black people are true Israelites exiled in 'Babylon,' the corrupt Western order, and destined for redemption and repatriation to 'Zion' (Ethiopia/Africa). It is decentralized, with no single authority, and spread worldwide largely through reggae.
dossier.cosmos: God (Jah) is one and immanent, present within each person — the doctrine of 'I and I' collapsing the distance between the human and the divine. Divinity is historical and embodied, revealed in a living Black African monarch rather than a distant heaven, and history is read through the Hebrew Bible as Israel's exile and coming redemption. Salvation is this-worldly — liberation from Babylon and return to Africa within this life — and many Rastafari affirm an 'everliving' continuity that downplays death and a conventional afterlife.
dossier.practice: Worship centers on communal 'reasoning' sessions and 'groundations' built around the sacramental smoking of ganja (cannabis) as an aid to meditation, and on Nyabinghi drumming and chanting. Distinctive observances include the wearing of dreadlocks (linked to the Nazirite vow), an Ital natural diet, and the Iyaric ('Dread Talk') reworking of language. The movement is organized into several 'Mansions' — notably Nyahbinghi, Bobo Ashanti, and the Twelve Tribes of Israel — differing in emphasis and practice.
dossier.canon: No closed scripture: the Hebrew Bible read 'with African eyes,' the Ethiopian 'Kebra Nagast,' and foundational texts such as Robert Athlyi Rogers's 'The Holy Piby' and Leonard Howell's 'The Promised Key,' alongside an oral tradition of reasoning and the lyrics of reggae.
dossier.reflects: The refusal of a deferred, otherworldly salvation — the insistence that God is Black, living, and historical, and that redemption is concrete liberation and return within this life.
sources: Ennis B. Edmonds, 'Rastafari: A Very Short Introduction' (2012)
